summary: Stancil argues that data startups overhype “open standards” as the modern equivalent of network effects, but most so‑called standards are fragile and transient. Durable success usually comes from building useful products and brands, not from winning imaginary winner‑take‑all markets. He proposes a practical, paid “switchboard” for metadata plumbing rather than another aspirational standard.
money_quotes: Standards that are only standards until a better idea comes along aren’t really standards at all.
Network effects often make for better funding pitches than actual flywheels.
The rest is fable and folklore, the conflation of social proof and a high NPS with something much more mythical.
Open standards could solve this problem, if everyone consolidated around that standard. A snowball’s chance, as they say.
Just cold, hard product, sold for cold, hard cash.
key_insight: In the data stack, chasing open standards is mostly a pitch narrative; real value and money come from pragmatic products that solve integration plumbing without relying on network effects.
